message.content: For the languages which are just simple configurations, like in the following pull request, wouldn't it be more logical to have a way to set up any language segment just using configuration in Oh My Posh and not pollute our segment landscape? We just resolve any possible executable with a version using a generic language segment that exposes the properties set in this PR? https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/pull/7691

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- compound-engineering:design:design-implementation-reviewer: Visually compares live UI implementation against Figma designs and provides detailed feedback on discrepancies. Use after writing or modifying HTML/CSS/React components to verify design fidelity. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:design:design-iterator: Iteratively refines UI design through N screenshot-analyze-improve cycles. Use PROACTIVELY when design changes aren't coming together after 1-2 attempts, or when user requests iterative refinement. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:design:figma-design-sync: Detects and fixes visual differences between a web implementation and its Figma design. Use iteratively when syncing implementation to match Figma specs. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:docs:ankane-readme-writer: Creates or updates README files following Ankane-style template for Ruby gems. Use when writing gem documentation with imperative voice, concise prose, and standard section ordering. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:best-practices-researcher: Researches and synthesizes external best practices, documentation, and examples for any technology or framework. Use when you need industry standards, community conventions, or implementation guidance. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:framework-docs-researcher: Gathers comprehensive documentation and best practices for frameworks, libraries, or dependencies. Use when you need official docs, version-specific constraints, or implementation patterns. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:git-history-analyzer: Performs archaeological analysis of git history to trace code evolution, identify contributors, and understand why code patterns exist. Use when you need historical context for code changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:issue-intelligence-analyst: Fetches and analyzes GitHub issues to surface recurring themes, pain patterns, and severity trends. Use when understanding a project's issue landscape, analyzing bug patterns for ideation, or summarizing what users are reporting. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:learnings-researcher: Searches docs/solutions/ for relevant past solutions by frontmatter metadata. Use before implementing features or fixing problems to surface institutional knowledge and prevent repeated mistakes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:repo-research-analyst: Conducts thorough research on repository structure, documentation, conventions, and implementation patterns. Use when onboarding to a new codebase or understanding project conventions. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:agent-native-reviewer: Reviews code to ensure agent-native parity — any action a user can take, an agent can also take. Use after adding UI features, agent tools, or system prompts. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:architecture-strategist: Analyzes code changes from an architectural perspective for pattern compliance and design integrity. Use when reviewing PRs, adding services, or evaluating structural refactors. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:code-simplicity-reviewer: Final review pass to ensure code is as simple and minimal as possible. Use after implementation is complete to identify YAGNI violations and simplification opportunities. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:data-integrity-guardian: Reviews database migrations, data models, and persistent data code for safety. Use when checking migration safety, data constraints, transaction boundaries, or privacy compliance. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:data-migration-expert: Validates data migrations, backfills, and production data transformations against reality. Use when PRs involve ID mappings, column renames, enum conversions, or schema changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:deployment-verification-agent: Produces Go/No-Go deployment checklists with SQL verification queries, rollback procedures, and monitoring plans. Use when PRs touch production data, migrations, or risky data changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:dhh-rails-reviewer: Brutally honest Rails code review from DHH's perspective. Use when reviewing Rails code for anti-patterns, JS framework contamination, or violations of Rails conventions. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:julik-frontend-races-reviewer: Reviews JavaScript and Stimulus code for race conditions, timing issues, and DOM lifecycle problems. Use after implementing or modifying frontend controllers or async UI code. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:kieran-python-reviewer: Reviews Python code with an extremely high quality bar for Pythonic patterns, type safety, and maintainability. Use after implementing features, modifying code, or creating new Python modules. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:kieran-rails-reviewer: Reviews Rails code with an extremely high quality bar for conventions, clarity, and maintainability. Use after implementing features, modifying code, or creating new Rails components. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:kieran-typescript-reviewer: Reviews TypeScript code with an extremely high quality bar for type safety, modern patterns, and maintainability. Use after implementing features, modifying code, or creating new TypeScript components. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:pattern-recognition-specialist: Analyzes code for design patterns, anti-patterns, naming conventions, and duplication. Use when checking codebase consistency or verifying new code follows established patterns. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:performance-oracle: Analyzes code for performance bottlenecks, algorithmic complexity, database queries, memory usage, and scalability. Use after implementing features or when performance concerns arise. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:schema-drift-detector: Detects unrelated schema.rb changes in PRs by cross-referencing against included migrations. Use when reviewing PRs with database schema changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:security-sentinel: Performs security audits for vulnerabilities, input validation, auth/authz, hardcoded secrets, and OWASP compliance. Use when reviewing code for security issues or before deployment. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:workflow:bug-reproduction-validator: Systematically reproduces and validates bug reports to confirm whether reported behavior is an actual bug. Use when you receive a bug report or issue that needs verification. (Tools: All tools)
- compound-engineering:workflow:lint: Use this agent when you need to run linting and code quality checks on Ruby and ERB files. Run before pushing to origin. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:workflow:pr-comment-resolver: Addresses PR review comments by implementing requested changes and reporting resolutions. Use when code review feedback needs to be resolved with code changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:workflow:spec-flow-analyzer: Analyzes specifications and feature descriptions for user flow completeness and gap identification. Use when a spec, plan, or feature description needs flow analysis, edge case discovery, or requirements validation. (Tools: All tools)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service — even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer — your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

attachment.content:
- context7-mcp: This skill should be used when the user asks about libraries, frameworks, API references, or needs code examples. Activates for setup questions, code generation involving libraries, or mentions of specific frameworks like React, Vue, Next.js, Prisma, Supabase, etc.
- ast-grep: Guide for writing ast-grep rules to perform structural code search and analysis. Use when users need to search codebases using Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) patterns, find specific code structures, or perform complex code queries that go beyond simple text search. This skill should be used when users ask to search for code patterns, find specific language constructs, or locate code with particular structural characteristics.
- code-changes: Orchestration workflow for any task that ends in code changes: issue analysis, pull request review, feature implementation, bug fixes, refactors, or fleshing out an idea. MUST be invoked at the start of such a task, before reading or writing any code. Defines how to analyze first, gate on user approval, plan, pick the right executor model, delegate and supervise subagents, verify, and deliver.
- conventional-commit: Workflow for generating conventional commit messages following the Conventional Commits specification. MUST be invoked every time a commit is created. Guides construction of standardized commit messages with correct type, scope, description, body, and footer.
- golang: Go coding standards and conventions for this project. Apply when writing, reviewing, or refactoring any Go source file.
- markdown: Markdown formatting rules for this project. Apply when writing or editing any .md or .mdx file, including documentation and website content.
- powershell: PowerShell cmdlet conventions for this project. Apply when writing or reviewing any .ps1 or module file.
- writing-clearly-and-concisely: Apply whenever writing content a human will read — emails, documents, reports, documentation, messages, UI copy, commit messages, explanations, or any other prose. Applies Strunk's timeless rules for clearer, stronger, more professional writing.
- compound-engineering:agent-browser: Browser automation CLI for AI agents. Use when the user needs to interact with websites, including navigating pages, filling forms, clicking buttons, taking screenshots, extracting data, testing web apps, or automating any browser task. Triggers include requests to "open a website", "fill out a form", "click a button", "take a screenshot", "scrape data from a page", "test this web app", "login to a site", "automate browser actions", or any task requiring programmatic web interaction.
- compound-engineering:agent-native-architecture: Build applications where agents are first-class citizens. Use this skill when designing autonomous agents, creating MCP tools, implementing self-modifying systems, or building apps where features are outcomes achieved by agents operating in a loop.
- compound-engineering:andrew-kane-gem-writer: This skill should be used when writing Ruby gems following Andrew Kane's proven patterns and philosophy. It applies when creating new Ruby gems, refactoring existing gems, designing gem APIs, or when clean, minimal, production-ready Ruby library code is needed. Triggers on requests like "create a gem", "write a Ruby library", "design a gem API", or mentions of Andrew Kane's style.
- compound-engineering:ce-brainstorm: Explore requirements and approaches through collaborative dialogue before writing a right-sized requirements document and planning implementation. Use for feature ideas, problem framing, when the user says 'let's brainstorm', or when they want to think through options before deciding what to build. Also use when a user describes a vague or ambitious feature request, asks 'what should we build', 'help me think through X', presents a problem with multiple valid solutions, or seems unsure about scope or direction — even if they don't explicitly ask to brainstorm.
- compound-engineering:ce-compound: Document a recently solved problem to compound your team's knowledge
- compound-engineering:ce-ideate: Generate and critically evaluate grounded improvement ideas for the current project. Use when asking what to improve, requesting idea generation, exploring surprising improvements, or wanting the AI to proactively suggest strong project directions before brainstorming one in depth. Triggers on phrases like 'what should I improve', 'give me ideas', 'ideate on this project', 'surprise me with improvements', 'what would you change', or any request for AI-generated project improvement suggestions rather than refining the user's own idea.
- compound-engineering:ce-plan: Transform feature descriptions into well-structured project plans following conventions
- compound-engineering:ce-review: Perform exhaustive code reviews using multi-agent analysis, ultra-thinking, and worktrees
- compound-engineering:ce-work: Execute work plans efficiently while maintaining quality and finishing features
- compound-engineering:claude-permissions-optimizer: Optimize Claude Code permissions by finding safe Bash commands from session history and auto-applying them to settings.json. Can run from any coding agent but targets Claude Code specifically. Use when experiencing permission fatigue, too many permission prompts, wanting to optimize permissions, or needing to set up allowlists. Triggers on "optimize permissions", "reduce permission prompts", "allowlist commands", "too many permission prompts", "permission fatigue", "permission setup", or complaints about clicking approve too often.
- compound-engineering:deepen-plan: Enhance a plan with parallel research agents for each section to add depth, best practices, and implementation details
- compound-engineering:dhh-rails-style: This skill should be used when writing Ruby and Rails code in DHH's distinctive 37signals style. It applies when writing Ruby code, Rails applications, creating models, controllers, or any Ruby file. Triggers on Ruby/Rails code generation, refactoring requests, code review, or when the user mentions DHH, 37signals, Basecamp, HEY, or Campfire style. Embodies REST purity, fat models, thin controllers, Current attributes, Hotwire patterns, and the "clarity over cleverness" philosophy.
- compound-engineering:document-review: This skill should be used to refine requirements or plan documents before proceeding to the next workflow step. It applies when a requirements document or plan document exists and the user wants to improve it.
- compound-engineering:dspy-ruby: Build type-safe LLM applications with DSPy.rb — Ruby's programmatic prompt framework with signatures, modules, agents, and optimization. Use when implementing predictable AI features, creating LLM signatures and modules, configuring language model providers, building agent systems with tools, optimizing prompts, or testing LLM-powered functionality in Ruby applications.
- compound-engineering:every-style-editor: This skill should be used when reviewing or editing copy to ensure adherence to Every's style guide. It provides a systematic line-by-line review process for grammar, punctuation, mechanics, and style guide compliance.
- compound-engineering:feature-video: Record a video walkthrough of a feature and add it to the PR description. Use when a PR needs a visual demo for reviewers, when the user asks to demo a feature, create a PR video, record a walkthrough, show what changed visually, or add a video to a pull request.
- compound-engineering:gemini-imagegen: This skill should be used when generating and editing images using the Gemini API (Nano Banana Pro). It applies when creating images from text prompts, editing existing images, applying style transfers, generating logos with text, creating stickers, product mockups, or any image generation/manipulation task. Supports text-to-image, image editing, multi-turn refinement, and composition from multiple reference images.
- compound-engineering:git-worktree: This skill manages Git worktrees for isolated parallel development. It handles creating, listing, switching, and cleaning up worktrees with a simple interactive interface, following KISS principles.
- compound-engineering:proof: Create, edit, comment on, and share markdown documents via Proof's web API and local bridge. Use when asked to "proof", "share a doc", "create a proof doc", "comment on a document", "suggest edits", "review in proof", or when given a proofeditor.ai URL.
- compound-engineering:rclone: Upload, sync, and manage files across cloud storage providers using rclone. Use when uploading files (images, videos, documents) to S3, Cloudflare R2, Backblaze B2, Google Drive, Dropbox, or any S3-compatible storage. Triggers on "upload to S3", "sync to cloud", "rclone", "backup files", "upload video/image to bucket", or requests to transfer files to remote storage.
- compound-engineering:reproduce-bug: Systematically reproduce and investigate a bug from a GitHub issue. Use when the user provides a GitHub issue number or URL for a bug they want reproduced or investigated.
- compound-engineering:resolve-todo-parallel: Resolve all pending CLI todos using parallel processing, compound on lessons learned, then clean up completed todos.
- compound-engineering:test-browser: Run browser tests on pages affected by current PR or branch
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files) or Word templates (.dotx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', '.dotx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx or .dotx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:frontend-slides: Create stunning, animation-rich HTML presentations from scratch or by converting PowerPoint files. Use when the user wants to build a presentation, convert a PPT/PPTX to web, or create slides for a talk/pitch. Helps non-designers discover their aesthetic through visual exploration rather than abstract choices.
- anthropic-skills:itdepends-offering: Use this skill whenever Jan (IT depends) needs to create a professional offering, proposal, quote, or commercial document as a Word (.docx) file. This skill extends the base docx skill with IT depends brand styling: the company logo, clean sans-serif typography, minimal layout with light gray label rows, and structured sections (scope, pricing, timeline, terms). Trigger on phrases like "create an offering", "make a proposal", "write a quote document", "draft a commercial proposal", or "create a Word offering for [client]". Always use this skill (not the generic docx skill) when the output is an IT depends branded offering or proposal.
- anthropic-skills:morning: Render the user's morning brief as a styled HTML artifact, or set it up as a recurring weekday task. Use when: morning brief, daily brief, set up my morning brief, what's my day look like, run my brief.
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx or .potx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx or .potx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates (.potx), layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx or .potx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx or .potx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:proposal-iteration-agent: Use this skill to start an interactive proposal development session. The agent asks intelligent, context-aware questions to gather all necessary information for crafting a professional proposal: client details, scope of work, pricing strategy, timeline, deliverables, and terms. The agent iterates with you, refines content, and maintains a structured proposal framework that can be exported to the itdepends-offering skill once finalized. Trigger on: "start a proposal",  "help me build a proposal", "iterate on proposal content", "gather proposal requirements", or "let's develop a proposal together".
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:spilberg-docs-style: Use this style guide whenever creating a Word document (.docx) for Spilberg — reports, technical documentation, assessments, proposals, or any client-facing deliverable that should look like it came from Spilberg (the IT Development & Testing staffing & consulting brand, part of Vibe Group). Trigger on phrases like "Spilberg report", "Spilberg document", "make this look like Spilberg", "branded for Spilberg", or whenever the user is producing a deliverable to share with Spilberg's clients or candidates. This skill defines Spilberg's brand colors, typography, logo usage, and full-page layout (including the signature green footer wordmark) so every document is instantly identifiable as Spilberg's own. Always combine with the base docx skill for the underlying docx-js mechanics.
- anthropic-skills:vibe-group-docs-style: Use this style guide when the user asks to generate a document for Vibe Group.
- anthropic-skills:writing-clearly-and-concisely: Apply whenever writing content a human will read — emails, documents, reports, documentation, messages, UI copy, commit messages, explanations, or any other prose. Applies Strunk's timeless rules for clearer, stronger, more professional writing.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .xltx, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

package segments

import (
	"encoding/json"
	"errors"
	"fmt"
	"path/filepath"
	runtime_ "runtime"

	"slices"

	"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/cache"
	"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/log"
	"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/regex"
	"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/runtime"
	"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/segments/options"
	"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/template"
)

const (
	languageTemplate = " {{ if .Error }}{{ .Error }}{{ else }}{{ .Full }}{{ end }} "
	noVersion        = "NO VERSION"

	versionFlagArg      = "--version"
	versionFlagShortArg = "-version"
	versionArg          = "version"

	versionRegex         = `(?P<version>((?P<major>[0-9]+).(?P<minor>[0-9]+).(?P<patch>[0-9]+)))`
	versionRegexPrefixed = `(?:(?P<version>((?P<major>[0-9]+).(?P<minor>[0-9]+).(?P<patch>[0-9]+))))`
	versionRegexSemver   = `(?:(?P<version>((?P<major>[0-9]+).(?P<minor>[0-9]+).(?P<patch>[0-9]+)(-(?P<prerelease>[a-z]+).(?P<buildmetadata>[0-9]+))?)))`

	fileName = "package.json"

	asdfToolName   = "asdf"
	bunToolName    = "bun"
	dartToolName   = "dart"
	denoToolName   = "deno"
	dotnetToolName = "dotnet"
	fvmToolName    = "fvm"
	juliaToolName  = "julia"
	mojoToolName   = "mojo"
	nodeToolName   = "node"
	npmToolName    = "npm"
	phpToolName    = "php"
	pnpmToolName   = "pnpm"
	pythonToolName = "python"
	yarnToolName   = "yarn"
)

type loadContext func()

type inContext func() bool

type getVersion func() (string, error)
type matchesVersionFile func() (string, bool)

type Version struct {
	Full          string
	Major         string
	Minor         string
	Patch         string
	Prerelease    string
	BuildMetadata string
	URL           string
	Executable    string
	Expected      string
}

type cmd struct {
	getVersion         getVersion
	executable         string
	regex              string
	versionURLTemplate string
	args               []string
	envs               []string
}

func (c *cmd) parse(versionInfo string) (*Version, error) {
	values := regex.FindNamedRegexMatch(c.regex, versionInfo)
	if len(values) == 0 {
		return nil, errors.New("cannot parse version string")
	}

	version := &Version{
		Full:          values["version"],
		Major:         values["major"],
		Minor:         values["minor"],
		Patch:         values["patch"],
		Prerelease:    values["prerelease"],
		BuildMetadata: values["buildmetadata"],
	}
	return version, nil
}

type Language struct {
	Base

	projectRoot        *runtime.FileInfo
	loadContext        loadContext
	inContext          inContext
	matchesVersionFile matchesVersionFile
	Version
	displayMode        string
	Error              string
	versionURLTemplate string
	name               string
	commands           []*cmd
	tooling            map[string]*cmd
	defaultTooling     []string
	projectFiles       []string
	folders            []string
	extensions         []string
	exitCode           int
	homeEnabled        bool
	Mismatch           bool
}

const (
	// DisplayMode sets the display mode (always, when_in_context, never)
	DisplayMode options.Option = "display_mode"

content.thinking:
The `Language` base struct already handles most of what the user is asking about—it's a generic engine that resolves executables, runs version commands, extracts versions with regex, and matches against file extensions and project markers. Simple language segments like the GCC PR are essentially thin configuration wrappers around this, just setting extensions, tooling details, and templates. So the user's real question seems to be whether simpler language segments could be even more streamlined.

I should check if there's already a generic, config-driven segment pattern in the codebase (like a "command" segment for arbitrary shell commands) or if this design tradeoff has been explored before, since that would inform whether this is a viable direction or if there are known reasons it wasn't pursued.

content.thinking:
I'm seeing that each language requires its own enum entry, registration, Go file, and documentation—a lot of boilerplate that could be handled by a generic segment type using configuration for things like executable names, version patterns, and file extensions. I have enough to make a recommendation on whether this approach would actually be more logical.

My take is yes, it would generally work—the Language base class already handles most of the generic work, so a config-driven segment could absorb these GCC-style PRs. But there's a real tradeoff: template variables, icons, documentation, and language-specific quirks like multi-tool fallbacks or version file matching still need individual attention, and having named segment types gives users something discoverable and searchable rather than a generic config that's harder to validate and document.

content.text:
Looking at [language.go](src/segments/language.go) and [fortran.go](src/segments/fortran.go), you're largely right — the `Language` base already *is* that generic engine (executable + args + regex + extensions/project-files context), and something like GCC is genuinely just ~25 lines of config bolted onto it. A generic `language`/`executable` segment type that takes `tooling`, `extensions`, `project_files` etc. directly in the user's config would eliminate a lot of this boilerplate (new `.go` file + test + schema entry + mdx page) for the "one executable, one version regex" case.

The tradeoff: it works cleanly for simple cases, but several existing language segments aren't actually simple — they need custom `loadContext`/multi-tool fallback (asdf, fvm), version-file matching, or bespoke template fields (Node's `.PackageManagerIcon` (src/segments/node.go), Go's toolchain detection). You'd end up with a generic segment for the easy 70% and dedicated Go segments for the rest anyway, plus you lose per-language schema autocomplete/validation and a dedicated docs page unless you invest in generic schema tooling for it. I'd frame it as "add a generic executable-version segment for the trivial cases" rather than "replace the segment-per-language pattern" — worth raising as an issue/discussion on the repo before committing to it as a direction.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: D:\oh-my-posh\.claude\skills\code-changes

# Code Changes

The workflow for going from an issue, pull request, idea, or feature request to shipped code.
Follow the phases in order. Analysis always comes first; code comes last.

Each phase has a reference file with the full instructions. **Read the reference file when you
enter the phase** — not before, and never skip it because the phase "looks obvious".

## Roles

Assign work based on model capability:

| Role         | Capability tier                               | Owns                                      |
| ------------ | --------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| Orchestrator | Strongest reasoning model available           | Analysis, plan, supervision, verification |
| Implementer  | Mid-tier coder; small model for trivial edits | Executing one pinned, self-contained task |

Concrete model names per vendor (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google) and how to run the split in Claude
Code, GitHub Copilot, Codex, or IDE agents: [references/model-tiers.md](references/model-tiers.md).

When the current agent already runs on an implementer-tier model, skip delegation: plan and
execute directly, still following every phase. Never delegate to a model more capable than the
current one.

## The flow

1. **Analyze** ([references/analyze.md](references/analyze.md)) — root cause and scope,
   validated against the code, never against the report alone.
2. **Plan** ([references/plan.md](references/plan.md)) — pinned spec, task split, parallel vs
   sequential, workspace per task.
3. **Delegate** ([references/delegate.md](references/delegate.md)) — match each task to the
   cheapest capable executor.
4. **Supervise** ([references/supervise.md](references/supervise.md)) — monitor, unblock, and
   critically review implementer output.
5. **Verify** ([references/verify.md](references/verify.md)) — quality gates plus functional
   proof, never delegated.
6. **Deliver** ([references/deliver.md](references/deliver.md)) — conventional commits and an
   outcome-first report.

**Stop gate:** Phase 1 ends with reporting the analysis and proposed approach to the user and
waiting for a go. Skip the gate only when the user already gave the go in the request itself
("do it", "fix it and commit", "implement with Sonnet").

## Special cases

These entry points replace or extend Phase 1; the rest of the flow applies unchanged.

- Pull request review comments →
  [references/pr-review-comments.md](references/pr-review-comments.md)
- Issue triage → [references/issue-triage.md](references/issue-triage.md)

content.content:
1	# Phase 1 — Analyze
2	
3	Start every task here, no matter how it arrived: issue link, PR number, verbal idea, bug report.
4	No code gets written or edited during this phase.
5	
6	## Gather the full context
7	
8	- Issues and PRs: `gh issue view <n> --comments` / `gh pr view <n> --comments`, plus linked
9	  issues, referenced discussions, and any code the report points at.
10	- Ideas and verbal requests: restate the goal and constraints in your own words. If the request
11	  is ambiguous, resolve the ambiguity now — not halfway through implementation.
12	- Check for prior art: existing helpers, similar segments/modules, and past commits that touched
13	  the same area (`git log -- <path>`).
14	
15	## Reproduce before theorizing
16	
17	Reproduce the problem when possible. A reproduction turns the analysis from a hypothesis into a
18	fact and gives Phase 5 its verification case for free. When reproduction is impossible (platform,
19	hardware, credentials), say so explicitly in the report and mark the fix as unverified-by-repro.
20	
21	## Find the root cause in the code
22	
23	- Read the actual implementation. Never reason from the issue text, a review comment, or a stack
24	  trace alone — reports and bot reviewers are frequently wrong.
25	- Distinguish the root cause from the symptom. Fixing where it crashes is not the same as fixing
26	  why it crashes.
27	- State what the change should be, which files it touches, and what it deliberately leaves alone.
28	
29	## Output of this phase
30	
31	A short analysis report to the user containing:
32	
33	1. What is actually happening and why (root cause, with file references).
34	2. The proposed change and its scope.
35	3. What is intentionally out of scope.
36	4. Open questions, if any remain.
37	
38	## Stop gate
39	
40	Report the analysis and wait for a go before implementing. Skip the gate only when the user
41	already gave the go in the request itself ("do it", "fix it and commit", "implement with
42	Sonnet"). A go given for analysis is not a go for implementation.
43

  is ambiguous, resolve the ambiguity now — not halfway through implementation.
- Check for prior art: existing helpers, similar segments/modules, and past commits that touched
  the same area (`git log -- <path>`).

## Reproduce before theorizing

Reproduce the problem when possible. A reproduction turns the analysis from a hypothesis into a
fact and gives Phase 5 its verification case for free. When reproduction is impossible (platform,
hardware, credentials), say so explicitly in the report and mark the fix as unverified-by-repro.

## Find the root cause in the code

- Read the actual implementation. Never reason from the issue text, a review comment, or a stack
  trace alone — reports and bot reviewers are frequently wrong.
- Distinguish the root cause from the symptom. Fixing where it crashes is not the same as fixing
  why it crashes.
- State what the change should be, which files it touches, and what it deliberately leaves alone.

## Output of this phase

A short analysis report to the user containing:

1. What is actually happening and why (root cause, with file references).
2. The proposed change and its scope.
3. What is intentionally out of scope.
4. Open questions, if any remain.

## Stop gate

Report the analysis and wait for a go before implementing. Skip the gate only when the user
already gave the go in the request itself ("do it", "fix it and commit", "implement with
Sonnet"). A go given for analysis is not a go for implementation.

content.thinking:
Most of these are straightforward configurations mapping extensions to tooling with executable, arguments, regex patterns, and optional version URLs. Java adds a custom regex and an extra environment variable entry for JAVA_HOME. Haskell gets more involved with a StackGhcMode option that switches tooling based on its value and checks parent files. Clojure uses initialization logic to conditionally set the default tooling through a string array configuration.

Now I'm wondering if the tooling option override already exists in the current system — many languages might already support overriding the command list via a tooling option in the config JSON. I should look at how Language.Enabled() works and trace through how tooling configuration options are currently parsed, since this could mean the config-driven approach is partially already implemented.

The existing `Language` base class already supports overriding `extensions` and `folders` through config options, and the `tooling` map is configurable too—but only as a whitelist to enable specific tools that are already defined in the segment. The real gap is that the tooling map itself, which contains the executable names and their configurations, remains hardcoded in Go.

The solution is to add a new generic segment type that embeds `Language` and builds the `tooling` and `defaultTooling` maps from a new config option instead of hardcoding them—something like a `tools` option containing a list of objects with name, executable, args, regex, and version URL template. This way, the default tooling order comes from the config, and the existing `tooling` option can still be used to select or reorder tools at runtime.

Now I need to identify which segments could be fully replaced by this generic approach—those with just extensions and simple tooling entries with static regex and no custom Go logic—versus which ones need custom logic like Haskell's StackGhcMode or Java's specific handling.

Looking at the candidates: Clojure is mostly straightforward (its init() method that conditionally sets defaultTooling is actually redundant since loadTooling already handles defaults), Crystal, Elixir, Julia, Kotlin, Nim, and Lua are all simple cases with no complex fallback logic.

I've confirmed Fortran and GCC are simple too. Now I need to examine the remaining languages—Mojo, OCaml, Perl, PHP, R, Ruby, Rust, Swift, V, Vala, Zig, Dart, Dotnet, Node, Golang, and Python—to get the full picture of what patterns exist across the codebase.

Dart fits the simple category too, so I've got a solid list now. I'm tallying up the candidates that qualify as simple implementations—that's twenty languages including Fortran, GCC, Clojure, Crystal, Julia, Kotlin, Nim, Lua, OCaml, Perl, PHP, Swift, V, Vala, Zig, Rust, Elixir, R, Ruby, and Dart. The ones I'm keeping separate for custom handling are Java, Haskell, Mojo, Golang, Python, and Node.

I need to verify Dotnet's complexity level before deciding where it goes. I'm also realizing that some of these entries like Angular, Aurelia, Svelte, React, and others are really frameworks and tooling rather than language segments, so they're outside the scope of what I'm focusing on—I want to stick with actual language-based segments that match the GCC pattern. Tool-only segments like Bun, Deno, CMake, and Bazel might fit a broader executable-version pattern eventually, but that's beyond the immediate goal.

Now I'm thinking through the design changes needed. I should introduce a new segment type, maybe called "executable" or something similar, though I need to be careful with naming since "language" is already conceptually tied to the base structure. I'm settling on `language` as the segment type name since it's not currently used as a SegmentType string and aligns perfectly with the docs category structure, even though `executable` would be more technically accurate—the project's focus on versioned language toolchains with extension-triggered context makes `language` the better semantic fit. Now I need to figure out the config schema changes, likely adding a new option like `tools` to define an array of tool configurations. I need to expose the `project_files` option since it's currently a Go-only field, and the generic segment needs it for things like detecting config files. I'm also realizing I need a way to name segments for the cache key — currently using runtime reflection to grab the calling file's base name, but I should verify if that approach works across different segment types. Icons are handled by the user's template config directly, so that's not a blocker. For registration, I need to create a concrete `GenericLanguage` type that wraps the `Language` segment and implements the `Template()` method, which returns the default template but can be overridden by the user's config since `template` is a standard segment config field.

I also need to add schema definitions for the generic segment's options—the tools array with name, executable, args, regex, and version URL template, plus the existing project files and extensions—and write documentation explaining the configuration with a worked example using GCC. The trickier question is the migration strategy: whether to actually remove the existing ~20 language-specific Go files and replace them with bundled default configs. The second approach would keep the public API stable so existing configs don't break, but internally swap out those twenty bespoke Go files for instances of the generic engine pre-configured with each language's tool settings—this shrinks the codebase while maintaining backward compatibility, though it requires more refactoring and testing. The user seems to be leaning toward this option as the better long-term solution despite the upfront effort. I need to wrap up the analysis by writing the formal report in the required format: findings with file references, the proposed change and scope, what's out of scope, and open questions. I'll also provide a direct recommendation on naming — `language` as the primary choice given how it aligns with the docs categorization, though I'll note the alternatives in case the user prefers something else. Then I'll wait for the go-ahead before moving into the planning phase.

content.text:
## Analysis

**Why this is tractable:** [`Language`](src/segments/language.go) (the base struct every language segment embeds) is already a generic "run an executable, regex out a version, decide visibility" engine. `extensions`, `folders`, `display_mode`, `home_enabled`, `missing_command_text`, `version_url_template`, `cache_duration`, and even *which* of a segment's predefined tools to use ([`tooling` option](src/segments/language.go:137-138), consumed by `loadTooling()` at [language.go:215-229](src/segments/language.go:215)) are **already config-overridable**. The one thing that isn't configurable is the `tooling` *map itself* — the `{executable, args, regex, version_url_template}` tuples — which today is hardcoded Go per segment.

**Survey of `src/segments/*.go`** — of the ~26 language segments, these are pure config (extensions/project files + a `tooling` map + `defaultTooling`, nothing else):

Fortran, Clojure, Crystal, Julia, Kotlin, Nim, Lua, OCaml, Perl, Php, Swift, V, Vala, Zig, Rust, Elixir, R, Ruby, Dart — 19 segments, plus GCC from the PR that prompted this. All of them could be deleted as `.go` files and expressed entirely as config against a single generic implementation.

These need to stay bespoke — they branch on option values, read files, or read env vars beyond the tool-selection pattern:
- [java.go](src/segments/java.go) — builds a dynamic executable path from `JAVA_HOME`
- [haskell.go](src/segments/haskell.go) — `stack_ghc_mode` option drives conditional tool switching + parent-file check
- [mojo.go](src/segments/mojo.go) — Pixi venv detection (`PIXI_ENVIRONMENT_NAME`)
- [golang.go](src/segments/golang.go) — parses `go.mod`/`go.work` for the version instead of running a binary
- [python.go](src/segments/python.go) (225 lines) / [node.go](src/segments/node.go) (158 lines) / [dotnet.go](src/segments/dotnet.go) — venv/package-manager/SDK-file logic

## Proposed change

1. **New segment type**, e.g. `type Language struct { Language }`-style wrapper (needs a non-abstract name — see below) registered in [segment_types.go](src/config/segment_types.go), implementing `Template()` → `languageTemplate`.
2. **One new option, `tools`**: an ordered array of `{name, executable, args, regex, version_url_template}` objects, consumed in `Enabled()` to populate `l.tooling` and `l.defaultTooling` — the only piece the base engine can't already do from config.
3. **One small gap to close**: `project_files` isn't exposed as an option today (only `extensions`/`folders` are) — needed since some candidates key off files like `shard.yml` or `Cargo.toml` rather than extensions.
4. **`getName()` can't be reused as-is** — it derives the segment's cache key by inspecting the calling `.go` filename via `runtime.Caller` ([language.go:141-145](src/segments/language.go:141)), which breaks when there's no per-language file. The generic segment needs an explicit `name` field in config to key its version cache.
5. **schema.json + one new docs page** for the generic segment, plus updates to `segment_types.go`.

## Name

`language` — it's free (not used as a `SegmentType` string today), matches the existing docs grouping (`/docs/segments/languages/`), and reads naturally: `"type": "language"` with `"name": "gcc"`. `executable` was the alternative but undersells the extension/project-file-triggered visibility logic that's specific to this family of segments, not to arbitrary executables.

## Out of scope

Non-`Language`-based tool segments (bazel, buf, cmake, gitversion, mvn, helm, etc.) use a different pattern and aren't part of this. Python/Node/Go/Java/Haskell/Mojo/Dotnet stay bespoke.

## Open question

Two ways to land this, different blast radius:
- **A — additive only:** ship `language` for new/custom languages; leave the 19 existing simple segments' `.go` files untouched. Low risk, doesn't reduce existing maintenance surface.
- **B — migrate too:** keep the existing `type: "fortran"` etc. strings and docs pages working (no user-facing config break), but reimplement their registration to instantiate the generic engine from a small built-in data table instead of 19 separate structs — collapsing ~500 lines of near-duplicate Go into one table.

I'd lean **B** since your original complaint was PR/maintenance burden, and it's backward compatible — but it's a bigger, higher-verification-cost change than A. Which do you want?

lastPrompt: You are absolutely right. What would we then need to change, and which segments can we replace with a configuration instead? What would a good name be for that segment?

file.content:
# Phase 2 — Plan

Turn the approved analysis into an executable plan. The quality bar: an implementer-tier model
must be able to execute each task without asking questions.

## Pin the spec

Write the spec down before delegating anything. It contains:

- The decided approach — including decisions already made, so the implementer does not relitigate
  them.
- Files to touch, and the entry points to start from.
- Constraints: style rules, patterns to follow (point at existing code), performance or
  compatibility requirements.
- Verification commands the implementer must run locally (build, tests, lint).
- Explicit non-goals: what the task must NOT change. This is what keeps subagents from wandering.

## Split into tasks

- One task = one self-contained unit an implementer can finish and verify on its own.
- Mark which tasks are independent and which consume another task's output. Parallelize the
  independent ones; sequence the rest. When in doubt, sequence — a merge conflict between two
  parallel subagents costs more than the parallelism saves.
- Documentation updates belong to the task that changes the behavior, not to a separate task.

## Decide the workspace per task

- Main working tree: when the task depends on uncommitted local changes, or when you will review
  and commit the result in the current session.
- Isolated worktree: everything else, especially parallel tasks — they must never share a
  working tree.

## Output of this phase

A task list where each entry names its executor tier (see Phase 3), its workspace, its
dependencies, and carries its pinned spec.

I've confirmed that each SegmentType maps directly to its own struct instance, and now I'm exploring an alternative approach where instead of individual factory functions for each language, I'd use a single factory that looks up language configurations from a built-in data table and returns a configured language instance.

The challenge is that the current `getName()` method relies on a runtime caller trick to identify the language, which breaks when multiple languages share the same struct. I need to set an explicit name field during construction from the data table key and modify `getName()` to use that cached name if available, falling back to reflection only when necessary. Since `Enabled()` currently overwrites the name unconditionally, I'll guard that assignment to preserve the explicitly-set name for the generic ConfiguredLanguage.

Now I'm mapping out the implementation tasks, starting with the foundational work that everything else depends on. Defining the ConfiguredLanguage struct and its methods... Both approaches share the same underlying validation engine but diverge at the entry point: the built-in types pull their defaults from a data table and allow config overrides, while the new generic `language` type gets everything from user configuration with no presets. I'm considering whether to unify these by treating the data-table values as defaults that the generic type can also leverage.

The idea is to have a single `ConfiguredLanguage` struct where `Enabled()` first attempts a table lookup by name, and if that fails, falls back to defaults populated from the user-supplied `tools` option. This way the migrated segment types get their factory defaults from the table, the new public `language` type can override or extend those defaults via config, and everything flows through one code path with minimal new surface area.

Defining the struct and its methods... Continuing with the logic for when user-declared tools override the preset defaults, and then delegating to the parent Language's Enabled method. I'm realizing there's a naming issue here — for migrated built-ins, the `name` field needs to be set during construction from the registration closure, but the reflection-based getName() fallback becomes unreachable code. I should guard against empty names and require users to explicitly provide the name when using ConfiguredLanguage generically.

For the public generic type, I need to read the name from options as a fallback, using a new LanguageName option constant. This keeps the design clean and minimal — the constructor sets the name for built-ins at compile time, while the Enabled method can read from options for custom configurations. I should document the exact option constants, their string keys, and the new struct name in the plan without necessarily implementing it yet, so an implementer can follow the spec clearly.

Now I'm laying out the full specification: the registration table with all 19 languages plus GCC data sourced faithfully from existing files to preserve byte-identical behavior, the exact steps to delete old files and update segment_types.go, changes to schema.json and docs, and the test strategy. The tricky part is that deleting the old language structs and replacing them with ConfiguredLanguage breaks existing tests that reference them directly — I need to decide whether to keep thin type aliases or update all the test files. The cleaner approach is to consolidate all language segment tests into a single table-driven test file that iterates through the language definitions, verifying each one produces the correct enabled/version behavior with mocked environments and commands — essentially reusing the test fixtures from the old per-language files. With 19 segments to migrate, each with their own test file covering tool fallbacks and version regexes, this is substantial work that can be parallelized across multiple tasks.

I'll structure this as Task 1 (sequential foundation) to build the generic engine — modifying the language file to guard the name call and add configuration options, then creating a new configured language file with the struct, template method, enabled logic, tools parser, and the full language definitions table pre-populated for all 20 candidates.

The test file will use table-driven tests covering representative cases like Fortran, Ruby with multiple tools, R with fallback logic, and Elixir with asdf to validate both simple and complex scenarios.

For tasks 2-5, I'll parallelize the remaining language migrations by splitting the work: Task 1 builds the core engine and seeds the table with just a few proof-of-concept entries, then tasks 2 onward each handle a batch of languages by adding their table entries, removing old implementation files, and updating the registration in segment_types.go along with schema.json and documentation.

Since the existing options like `tooling` and `extensions` don't change during migration, schema.json updates are minimal—the schema definitions for migrated types should remain largely unchanged unless we decide to expose the new `tools` or `project_files` fields there. I still need to create documentation pages for each language.

The core migration work per language boils down to five steps: add a table entry, remove the old .go file, replace the old test file with equivalent coverage, update the one-line registration in segment_types.go, and verify tests and linting pass. With 19 languages to migrate, I'm planning to batch them into roughly four parallel tasks of five languages each, but there's a merge-conflict risk since all tasks touch the shared table in segment_types.go and configured_language.go—I'll need to coordinate carefully to avoid conflicts.

I'm weighing two approaches: sequencing all migrations one after another (simpler and safer but slower), or restructuring the shared table to split it across multiple files using Go's init functions, which would let me parallelize without colliding on the map literal itself, though the segment_types.go registration lines would still conflict since they're all in the same block. after another by Sonnet, with me supervising between batches. For cost efficiency, I'll use Sonnet 5 across all tasks since the migration work requires careful regex handling and test rewriting — not trivial enough for a cheaper model. The final step is a full verification pass: building and testing the Go code, running the linter, confirming segment_types.go and schema.json stay in sync, verifying documentation builds, then preparing a conventional commit for handoff.

I also need to include the new public `language` segment type that was in the original request — a generic type for future GCC-style needs. This means Task 1 should cover both migrating the 19 existing simple segment types onto the shared engine and exposing the new `language` type with its full set of options (name, tools, extensions, project_files, folders), schema entry, and documentation example. I'll structure this as Task 1 for the core engine work, since both use the same underlying machinery.

For the implementation, I'm modifying `language.go` to add guards and new configuration options, then creating a new `ConfiguredLanguage` struct with methods for checking if it's enabled and parsing tools. I'll seed the initial language definitions map with two proof-of-concept entries — Fortran as a simple single-tool case and Ruby as a multi-tool case with fallback and post-processing quirks to validate the pattern handles edge cases properly. Then I'm writing comprehensive table-driven tests for both, and finally removing the old individual language files while updating their registration lines in `segment_types.go` to use the new shared engine.

For the second task, I need to add a public generic `language` segment type that registers in `segment_types.go` without a preset name, letting the `name` and `tools` options drive the behavior entirely. Then I'll document the new segment schema in `schema.json` with the required `name` field and other configuration options. Tasks 3–6 involve migrating the remaining 17 languages across four sequential batches, each containing 4–5 languages, where each batch depends on completing the previous one. For each language, I'm adding its configuration to the `languageDefinitions` table by copying over the exact extensions, tooling, regex patterns, and version URL templates from the original `.go` files, then removing those files and replacing them with equivalent table-driven tests.

After each batch, I'm verifying the changes by running the build, test suite, and linter. The batches are sequenced rather than parallelized because they all modify the same `languageDefinitions` map and the same section of `segment_types.go`, which would cause conflicts if worked on simultaneously.

Task 7 is the final verification and delivery step, which I handle directly without delegation — running the full test suite, linting, building the schema, validating `themes/schema.json`, spot-checking a few live segments in a real shell, and then committing the work with a conventional commit message.

For the commits themselves, I'll follow the repo's history-rewrite convention — likely one commit for the engine and public type, then one commit per migration batch, or squash them depending on what the repo's history shows as standard practice.

Now, since the user specifically asked me to review this plan with Fable 5, I need to invoke the Agent tool with the Fable model to get a critical review of the plan before finalizing it. I'll run this review in the foreground since I need the feedback before finalizing the plan for the user. I'm also pulling together the exact field data for the languageDefinitions table, verifying I've captured all the language file details correctly across the 19 languages I'm including. Specifying that the table fields must be copied verbatim from the existing `Enabled()` method being deleted is safer than transcribing complex regexes manually. But Ruby has a special case where `Full` gets post-processed to an empty string, and the shared `ConfiguredLanguage.Enabled()` method doesn't provide a hook for per-language quirks like that.

I could either exclude Ruby from the migration or add an optional `sanitizeVersion` function field to the table definition that gets applied after `Enabled()` returns — this keeps it explicit and lets Ruby stay migrated without pretending it's pure config. I'll spec this as a small detail so the implementer doesn't accidentally drop it.

Zig also has an extra exported method `InProjectDir()` that's only used in its own test file, so I need to check if that's safe to drop or if it needs special handling too.

content.thinking:
So `.InProjectDir` is actually a documented template field for Zig that's backed by `projectRoot`, which gets set when the project files match (like finding `build.zig`). It's not unused code—it's a real feature exposed to users. The interesting part is that `projectRoot` is already defined on the base `Language` struct and gets set generically by the `hasProjectFiles()` function, so Zig is just leveraging that existing infrastructure.

I'm considering two approaches: either expose this as a generic `.ProjectRoot` field or boolean on all languages (which would change Zig's template field name), or add an `InProjectDir()` method directly to the base `Language` struct so every language segment gets it for free. The second option is cleaner—it generalizes a Zig-specific convenience to all languages without special-casing, and languages without project files just return false harmlessly.

I'll update Task 1's spec to explicitly require moving `InProjectDir()` from zig.go to the shared `Language` struct before deletion, since Zig's docs already document this behavior and it needs to be preserved exactly.

Let me check back on that background bash task to see if it surfaces anything else, though the Grep tool already answered the main question.
